[{'id': None, 'title': 'Flexible All-Inorganic Perovskite Photodetector with a Combined Soft-Hard Layer Produced.md', 'content': 'Supporting Information
**Flexible All-Inorganic Perovskite Photodetector with a Combined Soft-Hard Layer Produced by Ligand Cross-Linking**

Tongyu Shi1,2, Xi Chen1,2, Rui He1\*, Hao Huang1, Xinru Yuan1,2, Zhenyu Zhang1, Jiahong Wang $I,2,3$ , Paul K. Chu4, Xue-Feng Yu1,2,3\* 

**METHOD**

Materials. The main chemical reagents including cesium bromide (CsBr, Aladin, $99.9\%$ ), lead bromide (PbBr2, Aladin, $99.0\%$ ), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Aladin, $99.9\%$ ), oleylamine (OAm, Aladin, $\mathrm{C}18{:}80{-}90\%$ ), oleic acid (OA, Aladdin, AR), 1H,1H,2H,2H-Perfluorodecyltrichlorosilane (FDTS, Aladin, $96\%$ ), chloroform (Sinopharm, $99.0\%$ ), ethonal (Sinopharm, $99.8\%$ ), acetone (Sinopharm, $99.0\%$ ), gold (Zhongnuo Advanced Material (Beijing) Technology Co., Ltd, $99.99\%$ ) were used without purification, except chloroform which was dehydrated before use. 

Synthesis of purified CsPbBr3 NCs. The CsPbBr3 NCs were prepared by the ligandassisted reprecipitation method. Firstly, CsBr (0.4 mmol), $\mathrm{PbBr}_{2}$ ( $\leftmoon.4\mathrm{\mmol}\right)$ ) were dissolved in DMF $\mathrm{[0\,mL]}$ ). Subsequently, OAm $\left(0.1\,\mathrm{mL}\right)$ ) and OA (1 mL) were added to stabilize the precursor solution until the powder was completely dissolved. 1 mL of the precursor solution was added quickly into chloroform ( $\mathrm{[0\mL]}$ under vigorous stirring, and strong green emission was observed immediately. After three times centrifugation ( $10{,}000\;\mathrm{rpm}$ , $10\;\mathrm{min}$ ), purified CsPbBr3 NCs can be obtained. 

Synthesis of CsPbBr3 $@$ FDTS NCs. The FDTS dispersant should be freshly prepared before use ( $10\;\mathrm{mL}$ chloroform, $30~\upmu\mathrm{L}$ ethanol, $1.5~\upmu\mathrm{L}$ deionized water and 10 $\upmu\mathrm{L}$ FDTS). Then $1~\mathrm{mL}$ chloroform and $1~\mathrm{mL}$ FDTS dispersant were added into the redispersed purified CsPbBr3 NCs chloroform solution ( $2\,\mathrm{mL})$ in sequence with a 5 min interval. After stirring for 10 min, $1~\mathrm{mL}$ chloroform and $1~\mathrm{mL}$ FDTS dispersant were added again. The obtained colloid solution was centrifugated (10,000 rpm, 10 min) to get the CsPbBr3@FDTS NCs, which were used for the TEM characterization (Figure 

1c) and photodetector fabrication. After further drying at $50\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the CsPbBr3 $@$ FDTS powders were used for other material characterizations and stability tests. 

Preparation of the CsPbBr3 $@$ FDTS flexible photodetector. The CsPbBr3@FDTS NCs were redispersed in chloroform to form a $10\,\mathrm{g/mL}$ uniform dispersion. Polyimide (PI) membranes $2.5\ \mathrm{cm}\times2.5\ \mathrm{cm}$ ) were cleaned sequentially in acetone, ethanol and deionized water. The CsPbBr3@FDTS film was obtained by the drop-casting method, and then the gold electrodes $\mathrm{\Delta}1\ \mathrm{mm}\times1\ \mathrm{mm}$ ) were deposited on the composite film by thermal evaporation, and the channel length between the Au electrodes is $25\;\upmu\mathrm{m}$ . 

**Characterization**

The morphology of nanocrystals was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200X) at $200~\mathrm{kV}.$ The composition was determined by energydispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) using the accessory manufactured by Oxford instruments. To characterize the structure, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Rigaku Smartlab 3kW X-ray diffractometer with $\mathrm{{Cu\K_{\alpha}}}$ radiation $\uplambda=$ 1.54056 Å, 40 kV, $30~\mathrm{~mA}$ , $10^{\circ}\:\mathrm{\min}^{-1}$ from 10 to $60^{\circ}$ ). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on the Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi system. The structure of the composites was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS60) with the KBr pellet method. The PL and TRPL properties were determined using a fluorescent spectrophotometer (Hitachi F4600 and Edinburgh FLS-1000) at an excitation wavelength of $365~\mathrm{\Omeganm}$ . The absorption spectra were obtained on the Lambda25 UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer. The morphology of the perovskite films was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss GeminiSEM 300) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker, USA). The mechanical properties such as elastic modulus and hardness were detected by a Nanoindenter XP (Bruker Hysitron TI950). Electric and optoelectronic measurements of the fabricated devices were conducted in darkness and recorded by the PDA FS380 semiconductor analyzer.

![](https://imgbed.siat-mic.com/i/2024/08/29/9be2fcdbd736c370f1681ef32686b0f30c8bd69e0fa0423560a454ac3083676a-1724930521.jpg)', 'metadata': None, 'embedding': None, 'score': None}, {'id': None, 'title': 'CsPbX3 Quantum Dots for Lighting and Displays RoomTemperature Synthesis, Photoluminescence Superiorities, Underlying Origins and White Light-Emitting Diodes.md', 'content': '

3. Conclusion
In summary, in this work, we report a facile SR method for the synthesis of highly emissive IPQDs. Emissions covering the entire visible region can be obtained via f ine controlling of composition and particle size. Excellent photoluminescence with narrow line-widths, high QY up to ${\approx}90\%$ and high photo stability are achieved, which are comparable with that synthesized by HI method. Detailed investigations about the inf luences of surface states and crystal structures on optical properties were made. The exciton binding energies are calculated to be 40 and $44~\mathrm{meV}$ for CPB-M and CPB-C, which are appropriate for the stimulated emission at RT. The Br rich surface states endow $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}{\mathrm{Br}}_{3}$ QDs with interesting anion exchange reactions, selfpassivating, and self-soldering behaviors. Finally, thanks to the outstanding optical properties, WLED with pure white color and high stability are fabricated. There is no doubt that this eff icient approach for the synthesis of the outstanding QDs will be in favor of a range of applications including lasing, lighting, and display. Though more investigations are needed to reveal the correlations between structural (especially the surface states) and physical properties, our f indings will provide good references and enhance researchers’ understanding of this QD system, pushing it to a new research paradigm in the f ield of optoelectronic devices, as well as sensors and memristors. 

purif ication. In a typical synthesis of $\mathsf{C s P b B r}_{3}$ , $\mathsf{P b B r}_{2}$ $(0.4\,\mathsf{\,m m o l})$ and CsBr $(0.4\,\mathsf{\,m m o l})$ were dissolved in DMF or DMSO $(10\ m L)$ . OA $(1\ m L)$ and $\mathsf{O A m}$ $(0.5\;\mathrm{\mL})$ were added to stabilize the precursor solution. Then, $\rceil\mathsf{\Omega}_{\mathsf{m L}}$ of the precursor solution was quickly added into toluene $(\mathsf{10\ m L})$ under vigorous stirring. Strong green emission was observed immediately after the injection. Other samples with different colors were fabricated with the mixture of $\mathsf{P b X}_{2}$ and $\mathsf{C s X}$ $(\mathsf{X}=\mathsf{C}\mathsf{I}$ , Br, I). All above operations were implemented at room temperature, which varies with seasons from about $30^{\circ}$ in the summer of Nanjing to about $0^{\circ}$ in the winter of Najing. 

Synthesis of $\mathsf{C s}P b X_{3}$ QDs with $H I$ Method : First, the Cs-precursor was prepared according to our previous report and stored in a glove-box before use. In a typical synthesis of $\mathsf{C s P b B r}_{3}$ , octadecene ( $.75m L$ , ODE), OA $(1.5\ m L)$ , OAm $(1.5\ m L)$ , and $\mathsf{P b B r}_{2}~(0.2~\mathsf{g})$ were mixed and degased under argon for $\intercal\ {\mathsf{h}}$ . To obtain cubic structure, the reaction temperature was raised up to $180\ ^{\circ}C$ and the system was kept at this temperature for more $20\,\mathsf{m i n}$ . After that, $\mathsf{\tau}_{\mathsf{m L}}$ of the Cs-precursor was swiftly injected into above system and the reaction was stopped immediately with ice bath. The IPQDs were washed with acetone and toluene via precipitation and redispersed in toluene f inally. When injecting Cs precursor below $740\ ^{\circ}\mathsf C_{\mathrm{r}}$ monoclinic structure was obtained. Other samples with different colors were fabricated with the mixture of $\mathsf{P b X}_{2}$ $(\mathsf X=\mathsf C|$ , Br, I). 

Fabrication of LED Devices : IPQDs/PMMA composites were prepared via dispersing the colloids into the PMMA/chloroform solution. Then, composite layers were casting onto the surface of a blue LED chips (centered at $460\ \mathsf{n m}$ ) and dried under vacuum at $60~^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $72~\mathsf{h}$ 

Measurement and Characterization : XRD patterns were recorded on a multipurpose XRD system D8 Advance from Bruker. UV/Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu 3600 UV/Vis spectrophotometer and photoluminescence spectra were measured with a Varian Cary Eclipse instrument. SEM images and EDX results were measured on FEI Quanta 250F. HRTEM images were recorded on FEI Tecnai G20. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a Horiba FM-4P time-corrected single-photon-counting (TCSPC) system. The temperature dependent photoluminescence spectra were measured at Horiba luminescence spectrometer (HR 320) with a laser excitation $(442\,\mathsf{\ n m})$ at different temperatures in a closed-circuit liquid helium system. The light source was focused onto the sample substrate by an immersion-oil objective and the PL signal of single-particle was collected by the same objective and a charge-coupled device camera. Meanwhile, the PL signal was sent through a beam splitter tube to two avalanche photodiodes in a Hanbury Brown-Twiss conf iguration for the PL decay measurement. The QYs of the samples were measured according to our previous reports. The reference dyes are quinine sulfate, f luorescein and rhodamine 6G. 

Computational Details : All the density functional calculations are performed by using the Vienna ab simulation package (VASP),[ 45 ] under periodic boundary conditions. The projector-augmented-wave (PAW) potential with cesium (Cs), lead $(\mathsf{P b})$ , and bromine (Br) is described as valence. Spin-orbital coupling (SOC) effect is induced because of Cs and Pb element. Geometry optimization and band structure calculations are carried out under the Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerholf (PBE)[ 46 ] exchange-correlation functional. The kinetic energy cutoff of the plane wave is set to be $500~{\mathsf{e V}}$ on a grid of $11\times11\times11$ k-points. The accuracy can be verif ied by the optimized $\mathsf{C s P b B r}_{3}$ lattice parameter a = 5.99 Å.[ 18 ] 

Synthesis of $C s P b X_{3}$ QDs with SR Strategy : All the reagents were purchased from Aladdin and were used directly without further', 'metadata': None, 'embedding': None, 'score': None}, {'id': None, 'title': 'Room temperature synthesis of stable silica-coated CsPbBr3 quantum dots for amplified spontaneous emission.md', 'content': '3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
$\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs were obtained through a modified supersaturated recrystallization method at room temperature, and the whole process only took $10\,\mathrm{\s}.$ . As shown in Fig. 1, first CsBr, $\mathrm{PbBr}_{2}$ , OA, and OAm ( $\mathrm{OA}{:}\mathrm{OAm}=3{:}1$ , volume ratio) were all mixed in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF). Precursors were then rapidly injected into the toluene solution, which contained a certain amount of 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES). Upon the injection into a poor solvent of toluene, $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs were formed immediately. Meanwhile, APTES gradually linked to the surface of QDs and then reacted with the trace of water from open air to hydrolysis [42]. Finally, silica $\left(\mathrm{SiO}_{2}\right)$ was formed and coated onto the $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs. 

The crystal structure of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}{\mathrm{Br}}_{3}$ QDs was first studied using X-ray diffraction. As shown in Fig. 2(a), black and red lines correspond to $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs, respectively. The $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs exhibit diffraction peaks at $15.66^{\circ}$ , $21.96^{\circ}$ , $31.01^{\circ}$ , $34.87^{\circ}$ , $38.15^{\circ}$ , and $44.30^{\circ}$ , which correspond to the (100), (110), (200), (210), (211), and (202) planes of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}{\mathrm{Br}}_{3}$ QDs (JCPDS 18-0364), respectively. The XRD patterns of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ are in good agreement with those of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs, indicating that the in situ growth of the $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ does not affect the cubic crystal structure of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}{\mathrm{Br}}_{3}$ . Surface functional groups were then determined using Fourier transform infrared spectra of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs shown in Fig. 2(b). While the weak peaks located at 1113, 1013, and $751~\,\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ indicate the presence of $\mathrm{Si-O-Si}$ bonds, the one located at $925~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ corresponds to the existence of Si–OH bonds, both suggesting the presence of $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ in $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs. To determine the chemical composition of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was also carried out with Fig. 2(c) showing the full scan of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs. While the peaks of Cs 3d [737.5 and $723.8~\mathrm{eV}$ , Fig. 2(d)], Pb 4f [142.8 and $137.9~\mathrm{eV}$ , Fig. 2(e)], and Br 3d $[68\;\mathrm{eV}$ , Fig. 2(f)] clearly demonstrate the formation of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ [43,44], Si 2p peak $[102.4\ \mathrm{eV};$ , Fig. $2(\mathrm{g})]$ and O 1s peak $[531.7~\mathrm{eV};$ , Fig. 2(h)] suggest the formation of $\mathrm{Si-O-Si}$ bonds [45] and are in agreement with the results obtained from FTIR spectra. 

![](https://imgbed.siat-mic.com/i/2024/08/29/6249d420dde0afd22d3ec6e22ed3afb62d8e8cf220988303c995309e1bad08b5-1724930879.jpg) 
Fig. 1. Reaction process schematics of the formation of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs. 

![](https://imgbed.siat-mic.com/i/2024/08/29/0519d0cc1a4cc16eada0cb196e5df4b3f053479e2a961328cdf1d59710c9f26e-1724930882.jpg) 
Fig. 2. (a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra, (c) XPS full scan of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs and the high-resolution XPS profiles of (d) $C s$ 3d, (e) Pb 4f, (f) Br 3d, (g) Si 2p, and (h) O 1s. 

Figures 3(a)–3(d) show the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the obtained $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}{\mathrm{Br}}_{3}$ QDs and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs, respectively. It is found that the $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs maintain an orthorhombic morphology, a good dispersity, and the same lattice plane distance of $0.58\,\mathrm{\nm}$ as pure $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs, indicating that the $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ coating has no effect on the crystal structure of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ . In addition, it was found that $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs were all dispersed and embedded inside the $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ . The average size of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs is $13.4~\mathrm{nm}$ [inset of Fig. 3(a)], while a shrinking and narrow size distribution with an average QD size of $12.3\:\mathrm{nm}$ is observed in $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs [inset of Fig. 3(c)]. This phenomenon might be due to the hydrolysis of APTES, which in turn leads to the presence of silica around the lead ions and binds the growth of perovskite crystals [46]. Figure 3(e) shows the energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping images of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs, from which a uniform distribution of the Cs, Pb, and Br components, and the presence of $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ were observed in the films with $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ . 

As shown in Fig. 4(a), although both $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs show green emission colors, the luminescence of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs is greener. In Fig. $4(\mathrm{b})$ , we show the PL emission spectra of both $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QD films with the same quantity, which were measured under the same condition. The PL intensity of the films with $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs is almost doubled compared with those using $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs. In particular, the PLQY of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs is as high as $71.6\%$ , while it is only $46\%$ in the $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ case. As shown in Fig. $4(c)$ , the absorption and PL peaks of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs are located at $529/526\ \mathrm{\nm}$ and 517/ $508~\mathrm{\,nm}$ , respectively. For $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs films, a blue shift is observed both in the absorption and PL results, which might be due to the decreased QDs size [47]. To understand the carrier dynamics, a PL lifetime measurement was carried out with the results shown in Fig. 4(d). These PL decay curves are fitted with a biexponential function consisting of a fast-decay component $\left(\tau_{1}\right)$ and a slow-decay component $(\tau_{2})$ [48]. The fast-decay component $\left(\tau_{1}\right)$ is speculated to be the trap-assisted nonradiative recombination, while the slow-decay component $(\tau_{2})$ is speculated to be the free-charge carrier radiative recombination [49]. Clearly, the $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs exhibit a longer decay time [7.6 ns $(\tau_{1})$ and 36.6 ns $(\tau_{2})]$ than $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs [4.9 ns $(\tau_{1})$ and 22.5 ns $(\tau_{2})]$ [in inset of Fig. 4(d)]. $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ might reduce the surface defect/trap state density of the perovskite QDs, resulting in a suppression of the defect-assisted nonradiative recombination [45]. Therefore, it is believed that such an enhanced PLQY and elongated PL lifetime of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs are mainly due to the passivation effects of $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ , and the decrease of the surface defects could contribute to the improvement of the QDs luminescent performance. 

![](https://imgbed.siat-mic.com/i/2024/08/29/a881fe885f003a13eb68153b913f6df3330a838b1cc7e3f52fda9fb60f25dc0c-1724930881.jpg) 
Fig. 3. (a), (c) TEM images and (b), (d) HRTEM images of pure $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}{\mathrm{Br}}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs. (e) Element distribution of $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs. 

![](https://imgbed.siat-mic.com/i/2024/08/29/005ddbad8d38755d0439317fdd9c11d6ea365e711767cbbdcc9ab45171e8df89-1724930883.jpg) 
Fig. 4. (a) Photographs of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs solution with/without UV light and films with UV light. (b) PL intensity, (c) Absorption and PL spectra, and (d) PL decay curves of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs. 

![](https://imgbed.siat-mic.com/i/2024/08/29/4115a526a3a1d86198bbaba64ad23fe8e4259c92a0d3769c5303562a2f572af8-1724930878.jpg) 
Fig. 5. PL spectra of (a) $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}{\mathrm{Br}}_{3}$ and (b) $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ . (c) Normalized PL intensity of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ under continuous heating at $60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . 

Subsequently, the thermal effects on the PL performance of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs films were tested, as shown in Fig. 5. While the PL intensity of $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs film decreases quickly under the heating at $60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ [Fig. 5(a)], the $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs film exhibits a slow decrease in PL intensity [Fig. 5(b)]. Although both types of the films show a decrease of PL intensity over time, the films with $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs maintain $84\%$ of their initial PL intensity, while only $24\%$ remains in the case with pure $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ after $80\ \mathrm{\min}$ continuous heating, indicating that the $\mathrm{CsPbBr}_{3}@\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ QDs film is fairly resistant to heat and possesses a good chemical stability. This result confirms that coating the $\mathrm{Cs}\mathrm{Pb}\mathrm{Br}_{3}$ QDs film with $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ could significantly enhance the thermal stability of perovskite, which is critical for optoelectronics in future practical applications.', 'metadata': None, 'embedding': None, 'score': None}]

To synthesize CsPbBr₃ nanocubes at **room temperature**, we can derive a suitable method based on the analysis of the retrieved documents. Here’s how we can synthesize CsPbBr₃ by selecting the relevant approach:

---

### Key Knowledge Fragments

1. **From Document 1 (Flexible All-Inorganic Perovskite Photodetector):**
   - CsPbBr₃ NCs are synthesized via a ligand-assisted reprecipitation method.
   - Cesium bromide (CsBr) and lead bromide (PbBr₂) are dissolved in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF).
   - Oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OAm) are added to stabilize the precursor solution.
   - The precursor is rapidly injected into a poor solvent (chloroform) under vigorous stirring to form nanocrystals.
   - Vigorous mixing leads to strong green emission (indicative of CsPbBr₃ formation).

2. **From Document 2 (CsPbX3 Quantum Dots for Lighting and Displays):**
   - A supersaturated retrystallization method is mentioned.
   - CsBr and PbBr₂ are dissolved in a polar solvent like DMF (or DMSO), followed by injection into toluene (a poor solvent) under room temperature.

3. **From Document 3 (Room temperature synthesis of silica-coated CsPbBr₃ quantum dots):**
   - CsPbBr₃ nanocrystals are formed by injecting CsBr and PbBr₂ in DMF into toluene (poor solvent) at room temperature.
   - Maintains nanocube morphology (though silica-coated versions are emphasized).
   - The role of DMF/toluene and stabilizers (OA/OAm) is critical for the formation of stable structures.

---

### Analyzing Relevant Methods

The ligand-assisted reprecipitation method and the modified supersaturated recrystallization methods described in the documents both align with the synthesis of CsPbBr₃ nanocubes at room temperature.

The steps described in documents indicate room temperature operations with stabilized DMF (or DMSO) solutions as a precursor injected into a contrasting solvent such as chloroform or toluene to prompt nanocube crystallization.

---

### Synthesizing CsPbBr₃ Nanocubes Using Ligand-Assisted Method (Optimal Approach):

#### 1. **Synthesis Conditions:**
   - **Temperature:** Room temperature (~25°C).
   - **Solvents:** DMF as the precursor solvent; chloroform as the reaction solvent.
   - **Stabilizers:** Use oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OAm).

#### 2. **Materials & Required Quantities:**

| Mat.ID        | Mat.Name             | Mat.Value/Range | Mat.Unit   |
| ------------- | -------------------- | ----------------| ---------- |
| M001          | Cesium bromide (CsBr)| 0.4             | mmol       |
| M002          | Lead bromide (PbBr₂) | 0.4             | mmol       |
| M003          | DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide)| 10          | mL         |
| M004          | Oleic acid (OA)      | 1               | mL         |
| M005          | Oleylamine (OAm)     | 0.5             | mL         |
| M006          | Chloroform           | 10              | mL         |

#### 3. **Equipment & Containers:**

| ID    | Name          | Param/Capacity      | Notes                         |
| ----- | ------------- | ------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| C001  | Beaker or Flask | ~250 mL            | To prepare precursor solution |
| C002  | Reaction Vessel| ~50 mL             | For injection and nanocube synthesis  |
| E001  | Stirring Setup | Variable speed      | Vigorous stirring required    |
| E002  | Centrifuge     | 10,000 RPM capacity| For nanocube purification     |

#### 4. **Synthesis Sequence:**

1. **Prepare the Precursor Solution:**
   - Dissolve 0.4 mmol CsBr and 0.4 mmol PbBr₂ in 10 mL DMF.
   - Add stabilizers: 1 mL OA and 0.5 mL OAm.
   - Stir until the reagents are fully dissolved and clear solution forms.

2. **Nanocrystal Formation:**
   - Rapidly inject 1 mL precursor solution into 10 mL chloroform in the reaction vessel under vigorous stirring.
   - Observe an immediate green emission (indicating nanocube formation).

3. **Purification:**
   - After nanocrystals have formed, centrifuge the solution at 10,000 RPM for 10 minutes.
   - Discard the supernatant and redisperse the nanocubes in chloroform.
   - Repeat purification steps at least three times to ensure high purity.

#### 5. **Characterization Methods:**
   - **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM):** Validate nanocube morphology and size.
   - **Powder X-Ray Diffraction (XRD):** Confirm crystal structure.
   - **UV-Visible Spectroscopy:** Analyze the photophysical properties.
   - **Photoluminescence (PL):** Study emission characteristics.

#### 6. **Safety Considerations:**
   - Use solvents like chloroform and DMF in a well-ventilated fume hood.
   - Maintain ensuring appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE), including gloves and goggles.

---

If you want, I can now outline the specific experimental details for each step. Let me know how you'd like to proceed!

To synthesize CsPbBr₃ nanocubes at room temperature, the ligand-assisted reprecipitation method can be used. Below is the detailed procedure:

---

### Synthesis of CsPbBr₃ Nanocubes

#### **Conditions:**
- **Temperature:** ~25°C (Room temperature).
- **Solvents:** DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide) as precursor solvent, chloroform as reaction solvent.
- **Stabilizers:** Oleic acid (OA) and oleylamine (OAm).

#### **Materials Required:**

| Material           | Quantity |
|---------------------|----------|
| Cesium bromide (CsBr)  | 0.4 mmol |
| Lead bromide (PbBr₂)   | 0.4 mmol |
| DMF                   | 10 mL   |
| Oleic acid (OA)        | 1 mL    |
| Oleylamine (OAm)       | 0.5 mL  |
| Chloroform             | 10 mL   |

#### **Procedure:**

1. **Prepare the Precursor Solution:**
   - Dissolve 0.4 mmol CsBr and 0.4 mmol PbBr₂ in 10 mL of DMF.
   - Add 1 mL of OA and 0.5 mL of OAm to stabilize the solution.
   - Stir the mixture thoroughly until all solids are fully dissolved.

2. **Nanocrystal Formation:**
   - Rapidly inject 1 mL of the prepared precursor solution into 10 mL of chloroform, which is under vigorous stirring in a reaction vessel.
   - Observe immediate green luminescence, an indication of CsPbBr₃ nanocrystal formation.

3. **Purification:**
   - Collect the formed nanocrystals through centrifugation at 10,000 RPM.
   - Discard the supernatant and redisperse the pellet in chloroform.
   - Repeat the washing and centrifugation process at least three times to ensure purity.

#### **Characterization:**
- **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM):** Assess nanocube morphology and size.
- **Powder X-Ray Diffraction (XRD):** Confirm the crystalline structure.
- **UV-Visible Spectroscopy:** Study photophysical properties.
- **Photoluminescence (PL):** Evaluate emission characteristics.

#### **Safety Precautions:**
- Conduct all procedures in a well-ventilated fume hood as solvents like chloroform and DMF are hazardous.
- Wear appropriate PPE, including gloves, goggles, and a lab coat.

---

This method ensures the successful synthesis of CsPbBr₃ nanocubes with controlled morphology and fluorescence properties, suitable for optoelectronic applications.
